edges.description: Tetramer dissociation exposes unstable monomers that misfold and aggregate into amyloid fibrils.
Extracellular deposition of misfolded transthyretin monomers as insoluble amyloid fibrils causes cardiomyopathy and polyneuropathy.
Amyloid oligomers and fibrils induce cardiomyocyte ROS generation and mitochondrial injury.
Amyloid infiltration perturbs calcium handling and electrical stability in cardiac myocytes.
Endoneurial amyloid deposition drives progressive axonal injury and demyelination.
Cardiac amyloid infiltration stiffens myocardium and impairs systolic/diastolic performance.
Cardiac amyloid deposition produces an infiltrative hypertrophic phenotype.
ATTR cardiac amyloid deposition can manifest as restrictive cardiomyopathy.
Cardiac involvement can include conduction disease and heart block.
intermediate_mechanisms: cardiac amyloid infiltration of conduction tissue

edges.description: Amyloid toxicity in peripheral nerves produces length-dependent sensorimotor neuropathy.
Peripheral nerve amyloid involvement produces progressive sensorimotor neuropathy.
Amyloid deposition in the transverse carpal ligament and flexor retinaculum compresses median nerve.
Enteric autonomic neuropathy and tissue amyloid deposition impair motility and absorption.
Gastrointestinal involvement can manifest as diarrhea.
intermediate_mechanisms: enteric autonomic neuropathy and gastrointestinal amyloid involvement

edges.description: Gastrointestinal dysmotility can manifest as constipation.
intermediate_mechanisms: enteric autonomic neuropathy and gastrointestinal amyloid involvement

edges.description: Gastrointestinal dysautonomia can impair gastric emptying.
intermediate_mechanisms: enteric autonomic neuropathy and gastrointestinal amyloid involvement

edges.description: Renal interstitial and vascular amyloid deposition contributes to declining kidney function.
Renal amyloid involvement can manifest as nephropathy.
Ocular transthyretin amyloid accumulation causes progressive vitreous clouding.
Vitreous amyloid deposits can be perceived as vitreous floaters.
Oxidative mitochondrial injury reduces contractile reserve and accelerates myocardial dysfunction.
Progressive cardiomyocyte energy failure contributes to symptomatic heart failure.
Calcium-handling instability promotes atrial and ventricular rhythm disturbances.
Impaired excitation-contraction coupling worsens pump failure.
Axonal loss and demyelination manifest as progressive polyneuropathy.
Sensory fiber degeneration causes distal loss of vibration and pain perception.
Irritable and degenerating peripheral nerves produce paresthesias.
Autonomic fiber involvement leads to widespread dysautonomia.
Autonomic denervation of vascular tone control causes postural blood pressure drops.
Autonomic fiber involvement can produce orthostatic hypotension due to dysautonomia.
edges.target: Orthostatic hypotension due to autonomic dysfunction

edges.description: Autonomic neuropathy can impair bladder emptying.
Combined neuromuscular and autonomic impairment contributes to chronic fatigue.

nodes.description: Misfolded transthyretin monomers undergo nucleation and elongation to form cross-beta amyloid fibrils. Serum amyloid P component and proteoglycans stabilize deposits and reduce clearance.

evidence.reference_title: Diagnosis and treatment of heart failure in hereditary transthyretin amyloidosis.
evidence.snippet: fibrillar deposits in a several organs including the heart, kidney, liver, and peripheral nerves cause organ dysfunction and associated morbidity and mortality.

evidence.reference_title: Familial amyloidotic polyneuropathy: protein aggregation in the peripheral nervous system.
evidence.snippet: It is possible that a common factor in the amyloidogenesis process exists among the different forms; this common factor can involve changes produced by mutations in the three-dimensional structure of TTR, rendering it prone to deposition as amyloid.

evidence.reference_title: Transthyretin amyloid fibrils alter primary fibroblast structure, function, and inflammatory gene expression.
evidence.snippet: Age-related wild-type transthyretin amyloidosis (wtATTR) is characterized by systemic deposition of amyloidogenic fibrils of misfolded transthyretin (TTR) in the connective tissue of many organs. In the heart, this leads to cardiac dysfunction, which is a significant cause of age-related heart failure.

evidence.reference_title: The Ultrastructure of Tissue Damage by Amyloid Fibrils.
evidence.snippet: Studies of nerve biopsy or cardiac autopsy specimens from patients with ATTR and AL amyloidoses show atrophy of cells near amyloid fibril aggregates.

nodes.description: Amyloid deposits disrupt intracellular calcium handling in cardiomyocytes, impairing contractility and promoting arrhythmogenesis.

evidence.reference_title: Epidemiology and clinical manifestations of cardiac amyloidosis.
evidence.snippet: The hereditary forms of ATTR have further come into importance with the availability of genetic testing and increased prevalence of certain mutations in African Americans.

evidence.reference_title: Management of Cardiac Symptoms in Amyloidosis.
evidence.snippet: Cardiac amyloidosis (CA) results in symptoms of heart failure, atrial and ventricular arrhythmias, conduction disturbances, and profound autonomic dysfunction.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical differences between transthyretin cardiac amyloidosis and hypertensive heart disease.
evidence.snippet: Patients with TTRA had higher levels of ultrasensitive troponin I (TnI-US) and N-terminal brain natriuretic propeptide (NT-ProBNP); in electrocardiography (ECG) they presented a pseudo-infarction pattern more frequently as well as conduction disturbances.

evidence.reference_title: Current and future circulating biomarkers for cardiac amyloidosis.
evidence.snippet: Cardiac amyloidosis (CA) comprises a heterogeneous group of medical conditions affecting the myocardium. It presents with proteinaceous infiltration with variable degrees of severity, prevalence and evolution.

evidence.reference_title: Pathophysiology of Cardiac Amyloidosis.
evidence.snippet: Cardiac amyloidosis (CA), usually caused by deposition of misfolded transthyretin or immunoglobulin light chains, is an increasingly recognized cause of heart failure burdened by a poor prognosis.

evidence.reference_title: [Carpal tunnel syndrome and ATTR-amyloidosis].
evidence.snippet: One cause for a carpal tunnel syndrome is transthyretin (ATTR) amyloid, which deposits in the carpal tunnel tissue.

evidence.reference_title: Orthopaedic Manifestations of Amyloidosis.
evidence.snippet: Common orthopaedic manifestations of amyloidosis include carpal tunnel syndrome, trigger finger, spontaneous distal biceps tendon rupture, rotator cuff disease, and lumbar spinal stenosis.

nodes.description: Constipation is a frequent gastrointestinal dysmotility phenotype in hereditary ATTR amyloidosis.

evidence.reference_title: Amyloidosis from the patient perspective: the French daily impact of amyloidosis study.
evidence.snippet: Patients often reported problems with mobility, usual activities, pain/discomfort and anxiety/depression, but not with self-care.

evidence.reference_title: Development of measures of polyneuropathy impairment in hATTR amyloidosis: From NIS to mNIS + 7.
evidence.snippet: It is a heterogeneous, multisystem disease with rapidly progressing polyneuropathy (including sensory, motor, and autonomic impairments) and cardiac dysfunction.

evidence.reference_title: Screening for ATTR amyloidosis in the clinic: overlapping disorders, misdiagnosis, and multiorgan awareness.
evidence.snippet: Amyloid transthyretin (ATTR) amyloidosis is a clinically heterogeneous and fatal disease that results from deposition of insoluble amyloid fibrils in various organs and tissues, causing progressive loss of function.

evidence.reference_title: Prevalence and determinants of iron deficiency in cardiac amyloidosis.
evidence.snippet: Amyloidosis is a severe and fatal systemic disease, characterized by an accumulation of amyloid fibrils in various tissues/organs, including nerves, kidneys, gastrointestinal tract, and heart.

evidence.reference_title: Outcome of gastric emptying and gastrointestinal symptoms after liver transplantation for hereditary transthyretin amyloidosis.
evidence.snippet: Gastrointestinal manifestations are common in hereditary ATTR amyloidosis and are important for the patients' morbidity and mortality.

evidence.reference_title: Fecal calprotectin levels are elevated in transthyretin amyloidosis patients with gastrointestinal manifestations.
evidence.snippet: Transthyretin amyloid (ATTR) amyloidosis is a rare systemic disorder characterized by amyloid deposits formed by misfolded monomers of the transthyretin. Gastrointestinal (GI) manifestations are common in ATTR amyloidosis; however, their pathogenesis is not fully elucidated.

evidence.reference_title: Severe chronic diarrhoea caused by hereditary transthyretin amyloidosis.
evidence.snippet: In the early stages of amyloidosis, gastrointestinal (GI) symptoms are uncommon. We describe a rare case of hereditary transthyretin amyloidosis (ATTRv) with involvement of the heart, nervous system and GI tract.

evidence.reference_title: Gastrointestinal manifestations of amyloidosis.
evidence.snippet: Gastrointestinal symptoms included anorexia, macroglossia, intestinal pseudo-obstruction, and altered bowel habits.

evidence.reference_title: Disorders of gastrointestinal motility associated with diabetes mellitus.
evidence.snippet: Gastrointestinal symptoms such as vomiting, constipation, diarrhea, and fecal incontinence occur frequently in patients with diabetes mellitus.

nodes.description: TTR oligomers and fibrils induce reactive oxygen species production and impair mitochondrial function in cardiomyocytes, contributing to contractile dysfunction and cell injury.
Amyloid deposition in peripheral nerves causes axonal degeneration and demyelination, affecting sensory, motor, and autonomic functions.

evidence.reference_title: Amyloid neuropathies.
evidence.snippet: Peripheral neuropathy is a common complication of many of the systemic amyloidoses.

evidence.reference_title: A Study of Familial Amyloid Polyneuropathy Induced by the TTR Val30Leu Mutation in China.
evidence.snippet: The clinical manifestations of this mutation involve mainly limb sensory or motor disorders or gastrointestinal symptoms or both, and the electrophysiological examination shows neurogenic damage.

evidence.reference_title: Development of measures of polyneuropathy impairment in hATTR amyloidosis: From NIS to mNIS + 7.
evidence.snippet: Hereditary transthyretin-mediated amyloidosis (hATTR amyloidosis) is a rare, life-threatening disease, caused by point mutations in the transthyretin gene. It is a heterogeneous, multisystem disease with rapidly progressing polyneuropathy (including sensory, motor, and autonomic impairments) and cardiac dysfunction.

evidence.reference_title: Functional and structural markers of peripheral microvascular autonomic neuropathy.
evidence.snippet: Autonomic dysfunction is a common complication of small-fiber neuropathy (SFN).

evidence.reference_title: Transthyretin amyloidosis and the kidney.
evidence.snippet: Peripheral neuropathy and cardiomyopathy are broadly described, and insights into disease reveal that kidney impairment and proteinuria are also clinical features.

evidence.reference_title: Albuminuria in transthyretin cardiac amyloidosis: Prevalence, progression and prognostic importance.
evidence.snippet: Transthyretin cardiac amyloidosis (ATTR-CA) is an infiltrative cardiomyopathy that commonly presents with concomitant chronic kidney disease.

nodes.description: Mutations in the TTR gene or age-related changes destabilize the native tetrameric structure, leading to dissociation into monomers. This is the rate-limiting step enabling protein misfolding and aggregation.

evidence.reference_title: A Brief Journey through Protein Misfolding in Transthyretin Amyloidosis (ATTR Amyloidosis).
evidence.snippet: Transthyretin (TTR) amyloidogenesis involves the formation, aggregation, and deposition of amyloid fibrils from tetrameric TTR in different organs and tissues.

evidence.reference_title: A Study of Familial Amyloid Polyneuropathy Induced by the TTR Val30Leu Mutation in China.
evidence.snippet: Familial amyloid polyneuropathy is currently prevalent worldwide as the transthyretin (TTR) Val30Met mutation, and there are other types of mutations.

evidence.reference_title: Genotype-Phenotype Correlations in ATTR Amyloidosis: A Clinical Update.
evidence.snippet: Hereditary transthyretin-related amyloidosis (hATTR) is the most common form of familial amyloidosis. It is an autosomal dominant disease caused by a pathogenic variant in the TTR gene.

evidence.reference_title: Transthyretin deposition in familial amyloidotic polyneuropathy.
evidence.snippet: hereditary transthyretin (TTR) amyloidosis which is a genetically transmitted disease that results from a mutation in the gene encoding the plasma TTR protein.

evidence.reference_title: Inhibition of the Amyloidogenesis of Transthyretin by Natural Products and Synthetic Compounds.
evidence.snippet: Hereditary transthyretin (TTR)-related amyloidosis is caused by mutations in the TTR gene. The mutations destabilize the tetramer and/or monomer of TTR, and thus the stabilization of TTR is a key strategy for the treatment of TTR-related amyloidosis.

evidence.snippet: We report on two elderly patients (an 80-year-old woman and an 83-year-old man) with progressive vitreous opacities (VOs) as the initial manifestation of hereditary transthyretin (ATTR Val30Met) carries, who had no evidence of systemic involvement or family history of amyloidosis and lived in non-endemic areas.
